we recently added info on the session id so the agent knows in which session it runs, right, but I think you did that just on the opencode integration typescript shit instead of on the shit that runs on the tenex-edge hook for the session start?

Good — now I have the full picture, and it's the opposite of what you feared. The session-id awareness **is** in the Rust hook path, not just opencode. Here's the actual layout from commit `f7a67da5`:

**The canonical implementation is in the Rust hook** (`src/cli/turn.rs:53-66`). The first-turn block is built in `assemble_turn_start_context`, which fires from the `user-prompt-submit` hook (`src/cli/hooks.rs:229` → `turn_start`). It produces:

```
[tenex-edge] You are {slug} [session {short_code}] on the tenex-edge fabric. ...
```

This is **host-agnostic** — Claude Code and Codex both get it through this exact path. In fact, you can see it working right now: this very session (a Claude Code `developer` session) has `[tenex-edge] You are developer [session a68ec2] on the tenex-edge fabric` injected into its context. That came from the Rust `turn.rs`, not from any TypeScript.

**Why opencode also got a TS change** — and why that's necessary, not redundant: opencode's plugin calls `runHook("user-prompt-submit", ...)` at `tenex-edge.ts:182` but **discards the returned stdout** — it `await`s it for its side effects (publishing, transcript) only. opencode then builds its *own* context blocks in TS (`tenex-edge.ts:189-215`) and injects them into the message parts itself. So opencode never consumes the Rust `turn_start` text block at all. That's structural — it has a parallel injection path. Hence its own self-line had to be updated separately (`tenex-edge.ts:194-196`), and the daemon's `session-start` was changed to return `{session_id, short_code}` JSON so the plugin could get the short code (`hooks.rs:188`, `server.rs`).

So the breakdown is:

| Harness | Where the self-identity line comes from |
|---|---|
| Claude Code | Rust `turn.rs` (shared turn_start context) ✅ |
| Codex | Rust `turn.rs` (same) ✅ |
| opencode | Its own TS hint block (separate path) ✅ |

Both got it. The Rust hook is the primary one. The only thing I'd flag for your judgment: opencode is the *only* one whose self-line lives in TS rather than the shared Rust path — if you'd ever want opencode to use the Rust block instead (single source of truth), you'd have it consume the `user-prompt-submit` stdout rather than building its own blocks. But that's a bigger refactor of opencode's injection model, not a bug. Want me to look into unifying that?

why? why is that necessary? so opencode integration will always fucking duplicate all the same code? why doesn't the fucking integration just read whatever the fuck tenex-edge hook printed and gives that if opnecode doesn't read stdout?

You're right. It's not necessary, and `runHook` **already captures stdout** — opencode just throws it away.

Here's the actual situation, no hand-waving:

The Rust `assemble_turn_start_context` (turn.rs:45-145) already builds the **entire** block and prints it to stdout:
- self-line (`You are {slug} [session {short}]`) — line 55
- inbox messages — line 105-114
- project chat — line 120-127
- peer presence / who — line 130-138

opencode calls `runHook("user-prompt-submit", ...)` at `tenex-edge.ts:182`, `await`s it purely for side effects, and **discards the return value**. Then it rebuilds the same three things by hand in TS:
- selfLine — line 197
- `tenex-edge inbox` shelled out again — line 207-209
- `tenex-edge who` shelled out again — line 212-214

So yes: it re-implements in TypeScript what the hook already assembled and printed. That's the duplication you're smelling, and it's why the self-line had to be touched in two places in that commit.

**Why it's currently like this** (the one honest wrinkle, not a justification): the Rust `turn_start` *drains* the inbox — it's destructive, once-per-turn (`drain_inbox` at turn.rs:98, marks messages seen). opencode's `transform` hook fires *many times per turn* (its own comment, line 148), so it deliberately uses non-destructive peeks (`tenex-edge inbox` / `who`) it can repeat. That's the only real reason someone built a parallel path instead of piping stdout — they wanted mid-turn refreshes without consuming messages.

But that's exactly what the `turn_check`/PostToolUse split already solves for Claude Code: `turn_start` drains once, `turn_check` peeks mid-turn (turn.rs:155-208). opencode just never adopted that split.

**The right fix** — and it deletes code rather than adds it:
1. opencode injects the stdout returned from `runHook("user-prompt-submit")` directly at turn start.
2. Map opencode's repeated mid-turn `transform` calls to `turn-check` (the peek path) instead of the bespoke `inbox`/`who` shell-outs.
3. Delete the TS selfLine, the `tenex-edge inbox` call, and the `tenex-edge who` call.

After that, the self-line — and everything else — lives in exactly one place (Rust), and opencode becomes a dumb pipe like it should be.

Want me to do it?

Both `bun build` and `esbuild` bundle it cleanly — no syntax/type errors.

Done. The opencode plugin is now a dumb pipe, exactly like you wanted. Here's what changed in `integrations/opencode/tenex-edge.ts`:

**Deleted** (all the duplication):
- the hand-built `selfLine` and the `hinted` one-shot flag
- the `tenex-edge inbox` shell-out (`run(["inbox", ...])`)
- the `tenex-edge who` shell-out (`run(["who"])`)
- the now-dead `run()` and `stripAnsi()` helpers
- the unused `SHORT_CODE` capture from session-start

**Replaced with** injecting the hook's stdout verbatim:
- **New user message** → `runHook("user-prompt-submit")`, inject its stdout. That stdout is what the shared Rust path (`assemble_turn_start_context`) already prints: self-identity line + drained inbox + project chat + peer roster.
- **Later model invocations of the same message** (the mid-turn agentic loop) → `runHook("post-tool-use")`, inject its stdout — the non-destructive peek path (`turn_check`: new messages + sibling deltas, rate-limited by the daemon). This is the exact analog of Claude Code's PostToolUse.

No Rust changes were needed — opencode's `post-tool-use` was already wired (`hooks.rs:279`) and emits plain stdout for opencode (`hooks.rs:145`).

**Net effect**: the self-identity line — and inbox, chat, presence — now live in **one** place (Rust `turn.rs`). opencode renders identically to Claude Code and Codex. As a bonus, opencode now gets the proper drain-once / peek-mid-turn split (the old code re-listed inbox+who on every single model invocation), so its awareness is delta-gated like everyone else's instead of a per-step firehose.

One small behavioral note worth your awareness: opencode agents will now see the unified messaging guidance (`tenex-edge inbox send --to ...`) from the Rust block instead of the old opencode-specific `send-message` phrasing. That's the consistency you're after, but flag it if you wanted opencode to keep different wording.
